channel.title: Theory of Computation on Megha Bose
channel.description: Recent content in Theory of Computation on Megha Bose

item.title: Automata Theory & FSMs
item.description: Prerequisites: none - this is the entry point for the theory of computation arc. When you type a URL into your browser’s address bar, a tiny program runs to check whether the string you typed is even syntactically valid. That program is a finite state machine. Every regular expression you’ve ever written - \d+, [a-z]*, (foo|bar)+ - compiles to a finite automaton before it runs. The traffic light controller at the intersection outside your building is a finite state machine.

item.title: Context-Free Grammars & Parsing
item.description: Prerequisites: Automata Theory & FSMs Every programming language you’ve ever used is defined by a context-free grammar. When Python tells you SyntaxError: invalid syntax, a parser running a CFG found that your code doesn’t match the grammar. When your browser renders HTML, it’s parsing a context-free structure. When a compiler figures out that 2 + 3 * 4 means 2 + (3 * 4) and not (2 + 3) * 4, it’s using the precedence hierarchy baked into the grammar.

item.title: How Compilers Work
item.description: Prerequisite: Context-Free Grammars & Parsing | Automata Theory & FSMs You write x = a + b * 2. The compiler turns this into machine code that: Evaluates b * 2 before a + b * 2 because multiplication has higher precedence Computes b * 2 as b + b using a lea instruction instead of a multiply, because addition is faster Eliminates the variable x entirely if it’s never used Inlines the function call if you wrapped this in a tiny function How does a text transformation tool - which is all a compiler is - produce code that is faster than most programmers could hand-write?

item.title: Turing Machines
item.description: Prerequisites: Automata Theory & FSMs Context-Free Grammars & Parsing In 1936, a 24-year-old Alan Turing was trying to solve a foundational problem in mathematics. David Hilbert had asked in 1928: is there a mechanical procedure that could, given any mathematical statement, decide whether it is provable? The Entscheidungsproblem - the decision problem. Could mathematics itself be automated? To answer the question, Turing first had to define what “mechanical procedure” means.

item.title: Decidability & The Halting Problem
P, NP, and Hardest Problems
item.description: Prerequisites: Turing Machines Decidability & The Halting Problem The Clay Mathematics Institute has offered a million-dollar prize for solving P vs NP since the year 2000. It remains unclaimed. Almost every cryptographer believes P $\neq$ NP - that encrypted data is genuinely safe from fast decryption, not just safe from decryption methods we happen to know today. Almost every algorithm researcher believes P $\neq$ NP - that the hard problems on their desks won’t suddenly become easy.

item.title: NP-Completeness & Reductions
item.description: Prerequisites: P, NP, and Hardest Problems In 1971, Stephen Cook proved that Boolean satisfiability (SAT) is NP-complete. One year later, Richard Karp published a landmark paper showing that 21 natural combinatorial problems - scheduling, graph coloring, packing, covering, partitioning - are all NP-complete, by reducing each one to SAT or to each other. The conclusion was striking: these problems, which had been studied for decades in operations research, combinatorics, and computer science, were all interconnected in a deep way.

item.title: SAT & Constraint Satisfaction
item.description: Prerequisites: NP-Completeness & Reductions Constraint Satisfaction & Backtracking In 2016, mathematicians Marijn Heule, Oliver Kullmann, and Victor Marek solved the Boolean Pythagorean triples problem using a SAT solver. The problem: can you color every positive integer either red or blue such that no Pythagorean triple $(a, b, c)$ with $a^2 + b^2 = c^2$ is monochromatic (all the same color)? They answered no, and their proof required checking a Boolean formula encoded across 200 terabytes of clauses.

item.title: Space Complexity
item.description: Prerequisites: P, NP, and Hardest Problems Turing Machines Sorting $n$ numbers takes $O(n \log n)$ time. But how much memory does it need? Merge sort needs $O(n)$ extra space - it copies half the array at each merge step. Heapsort needs $O(1)$ - it works entirely in place. These are very different machines. One could run on a tiny embedded device with barely any RAM; the other can’t.

item.title: Complexity Classes
item.description: Prerequisites: Space Complexity P, NP, and Hardest Problems Computer scientists have catalogued hundreds of complexity classes. P, NP, coNP, BPP, ZPP, RP, #P, PP, PSPACE, EXPTIME, PH, IP, PPAD - the list keeps growing. This isn’t taxonomy for its own sake. Each class captures a different kind of resource or computational capability. Understanding where your problem sits in this landscape tells you what tools are appropriate and, crucially, what limitations are fundamental.

item.title: Graph Coloring Algorithms
item.description: Prerequisites: Graph Algorithms & Traversal NP-Completeness & Reductions In 1852, Francis Guthrie tried to color a map of England’s counties so that no two adjacent counties shared the same color. He noticed four colors always seemed to be enough - no matter how complicated the map, he couldn’t find a counterexample requiring five. He asked his brother, who asked his professor, who asked nobody else for 124 years.
